---
title: "06 · Multi-object tracking"
subtitle: "Same object across frames — YOLO detection + ByteTrack"
---

::: {.callout-note title="New here? Read this first" collapse="true"}
No prior knowledge needed — but this report is easier if you've skimmed
**[Foundations](../../foundations.qmd)** (how a computer sees an image, what a model is, how we
measure success). Stuck on a word? See the **[Glossary](../../glossary.qmd)**. Stuck on a model
name? See **[Model families](../../models.qmd)**. Key terms are explained inline the first time they
appear.
:::

## The task

A **detector** (the model that finds objects and draws a **bounding box** — the rectangle around
each one) answers *"what's here right now"* — independently, every frame, with no memory.
**Tracking** (also called **multi-object tracking**, or **MOT** — following the same objects across
video frames so each keeps a stable id) adds the missing dimension: **identity over time**, so the
same person keeps the same **id** (the persistent number identifying one tracked object across
frames — also called a **tracker id**) as they move. That id is what lets you count, measure dwell
time, analyse motion, or have a robot follow a specific person.

The dominant recipe is **tracking-by-detection** (run a detector each frame, then link this frame's
boxes to the tracks you already have):

1. Run a detector on each frame (here: YOLO26 — a fast, widely used object-detection model; see
   [Model families](../../models.qmd) — person class only).
2. **Associate** this frame's boxes with the existing tracks — i.e. decide which new box belongs to
   which existing track — matching by predicted position (a **Kalman / motion model**: a simple
   predictor of where a tracked object will move next) and box overlap.
3. Births/deaths: unmatched detections start new tracks; tracks unseen for a while are retired.

**ByteTrack** is the associator. (It is an *algorithm* — a fixed set of matching rules — not a
neural network that learns; see [Model families](../../models.qmd).) Its trick is to use
*low*-confidence detections during association (not just high-confidence ones), which recovers
objects through brief **occlusion** (when an object is temporarily hidden behind something) and
reduces dropped tracks.

## Result

![Detection + ByteTrack with motion traces. Each box carries a persistent id (`#n`); the tails show recent trajectory.](results/tracking.gif){#fig-track}

{{< include results/metrics.md >}}

![Three frames across the clip — note how ids persist as people move.](results/keyframes.png){#fig-keyframes}

::: {.callout-note title="What to notice"}
- **Identity is the product, not boxes.** The same code as module 01 produces the boxes; the
  value here is that box `#7` in frame 0 is *the same person* as box `#7` in frame 100.
- **More ids than people = fragmentation.** This 200-frame clip yielded ~60 ids for far fewer
  actual pedestrians. When someone is occluded and reappears, the tracker often issues a *new*
  id — the classic **ID-switch / fragmentation** failure (a tracked object wrongly getting a *new*
  id, e.g. after being hidden) that HOTA and IDF1 are designed to
  penalise. (**HOTA**, **MOTA** and **IDF1** are standard tracking scores for how well ids stay
  correct over time.)
- **Tracking is detector-bound.** Every missed detection is a gap the associator must bridge;
  a better detector usually means a better tracker, for free.
:::

## Where tracking fails

- **Long / full occlusion** — a person hidden for many frames usually returns with a new id.
- **Crowds & similar appearance** — ByteTrack uses motion + overlap, not appearance, so it can
  swap ids between people who cross paths (appearance-based trackers like Deep OC-SORT help).
- **Fast motion / low frame-rate** — the Kalman prediction drifts and association breaks.
- **Detector misses** — propagate directly into track gaps.

## Reproduce

Here **supervision** is a Python helper library that bundles ready-made tracking and annotation
tools (including a ByteTrack implementation); see [Model families](../../models.qmd).

```bash
uv sync --group detection      # ultralytics + supervision (ByteTrack)
uv run python modules/06-tracking/run.py
```

*(The sample clip is downloaded by `supervision`'s asset helper into `data/`.)*
